data.title: Which under-the-radar winners have the best chance for further gains?
data.description:
Looking for smaller stocks that have attracted strong investor interest so far in 2025, but are still positioned for further gains?

The list below is meant to focus on those names. It contains stocks with a market cap of $1B or less and have climbed at least 50% so far in 2025. All the names also have Buy or Strong Buy ratings from both Wall Street analysts and Seeking Alpha analysts. 

The stocks on the list are then ranked according to their Seeking Alpha Quant Ratings. This system of grading stocks is based on their relative performance on key quantitative measures, like valuation, profitability and momentum.

Here is the list, with each stock listed along with its Quant Rating and year-to-date performance:

  • Innovative Solutions and Support (NASDAQ:ISSC) Quant Rating: 4.98; YTD Performance: +104.22%
  • Atai Life Sciences (NASDAQ:ATAI) Quant Rating: 4.98; YTD Performance: +211.28%
  • Strattec Security (NASDAQ:STRT) Quant Rating: 4.95; YTD Performance: +57.50%
  • Heritage Insurance Holdings (NYSE:HRTG) Quant Rating: 4.93; YTD Performance: +79.01%
  • CuriosityStream (NASDAQ:CURI) Quant Rating: 4.91; YTD Performance: +207.84%
  • Amylyx Pharmaceuticals (NASDAQ:AMLX) Quant Rating: 4.90; YTD Performance: +116.40%
  • Avino Silver & Gold Mines (NYSE:ASM) Quant Rating: 4.82; YTD Performance: +298.41%
  • Nutex Health (NASDAQ:NUTX) Quant Rating: 4.82 187.63%
  • United States Antimony (NYSE:UAMY) Rating: 4.72; YTD Performance: +94.35%
  • Oportun Financial (NASDAQ:OPRT) Quant Rating: 4.67; YTD Performance: +63.14%

data.title: Arhaus soars after 'resilient high-end customers' support sales growth
data.description:
Arhaus (NASDAQ:ARHS) was one of the biggest gainers in the consumer sector in Thursday morning trading after the retailer topped expectations with its second quarter earnings report.

Revenue was up 15.7% year-over-year to a record level for Q2 of $358 million. Comparable growth was 10.5%, driven by the successful conversion of strong first-quarter demand, while demand comparable growth was -3.6%, reflecting macroeconomic volatility and external headwinds.

"We successfully brought operations of our Dallas Distribution Center in-house which ramped ahead of schedule. This transition enabled us to convert strong first quarter demand into net revenue more efficiently, and at a higher volume, than expected," highlighted CEO John Reed. "With a resilient high-end client base, a debt-free balance sheet, and a clear strategic plan, we are navigating the current environment from a position of strength," he added.

During the quarter, the company completed 3 showroom projects, including 2 relocations and 1 renovation.

Arhaus (NASDAQ:ARHS) ended the quarter with no long-term debt and a cash position of $235 million.

Looking ahead, Arhaus (ARHS) noted that demand comparable growth in July was up 15.7%, reflecting strong client engagement and the strength of the Arhaus product assortment. Full-year revenue of $1.29 billion to $1.38 billion is anticipated, and adjusted EBITDA is seen landing in a range of $123 million to $145 million.

Shares of Arhaus (ARHS) were up 21.1% in Thursday morning trading.

data.title: Biggest stock movers Thursday: ABNB, DKNG, LLY, PTON, DUO, and more
data.description:
Stock futures edged higher on Thursday as investors weighed President Donald Trump's newly announced 100% tariff on imported semiconductors, exempting companies manufacturing in the U.S.

Here are some of Thursday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • DraftKings (NASDAQ:DKNG) +7% – Shares jumped after the company reported mixed Q2 results but delivered upbeat guidance. Revenue rose 37.3% Y/Y, while monthly unique paying customers increased 6% to 3.3M. Average revenue per user climbed 29%, driven by higher sportsbook hold and improved promotional efficiency. DraftKings posted record adjusted EBITDA of $301M vs. $243.8M consensus. The company reaffirmed FY2025 revenue guidance of $6.2B–$6.4B (midpoint: $6.3B), with expectations leaning toward the higher end as it eyes a new state launch.
  • Peloton (PTON) +10% – Shares surged after the company reported only its second quarterly profit since 2021, fueled by deep cost cuts across marketing, R&D, and overall operations that offset continued revenue and subscriber declines. Despite a 6% drop in Q4 revenue to $606.9M and falling connected fitness subscriptions, results topped expectations. However, Peloton guided for further revenue and subscriber declines in the current quarter and FY2026 while also launching a new restructuring plan targeting $100M in cost savings.
  • Duolingo (DUOL) +33% – Shares surged after the language-learning platform raised its full-year guidance on the back of strong user growth fueled by AI integration. Revenue rose 41% Y/Y to $252M, beating the $241M consensus, while net income jumped 84% to $45M, or $0.91 per share. Daily active users climbed 40% Y/Y to nearly 48M. The company now expects Q3 revenue of $257M–$261M vs. $253.34M estimated and lifted its FY revenue forecast to $1.01B–$1.02B (prior: $987M–$996M) vs. the consensus of $996.50M, with bookings seen at $1.15B–$1.16B.

Biggest stock losers

  • Airbnb (NASDAQ:ABNB) -6% – Shares dipped after the company’s conservative Q3 outlook overshadowed upbeat Q2 results. While Q3 revenue is projected to grow 8–10% to $4.02B–$4.10B, aligning with the $4.05B consensus, the pace marks a slowdown from 13% growth in Q2. Airbnb expects over $2B in adjusted EBITDA for the full year, but at a lower margin of 34.5% due to increased spending on growth and policy initiatives. The company also flagged similar Y/Y margin declines for Q4 and expects Q3 nights and seats booked to stay “relatively stable” Q/Q.
  • Lyft (NASDAQ:LYFT) -4% – Shares came under pressure following mixed Q2 results, as revenue, despite rising 11% Y/Y, narrowly missed expectations. Gross bookings hit a record $4.5B, marking the 10th straight quarter of new highs, while active riders posted a ninth consecutive quarter of double-digit growth. For the current quarter, Lyft guided for gross bookings of $4.65B–$4.8B (above estimates), adjusted EBITDA of $125M–$145M, and EBITDA margin of 2.7%–3.0%.
  • e.l.f. Beauty (NYSE:ELF) -11% –  Shares dropped after the cosmetics company reported Q2 results and issued cautious guidance for the first half of the fiscal year. The company now expects adjusted EBITDA margins of around 20%, down from approximately 23% in the prior-year period. Management declined to provide a full-year fiscal 2026 outlook, citing uncertainty around tariffs and a wide range of possible outcomes.
  • Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY) -13% – Shares dropped sharply after the company released topline results from a Phase 3 trial of its oral obesity drug orforglipron. Despite hitting all primary and key secondary endpoints, investors appeared underwhelmed by the weight loss data, especially relative to high market expectations. In contrast, rival Novo Nordisk (NVO) +11% rallied as the market interpreted the data as less threatening to its dominant position in the GLP-1 obesity drug space. Lilly also reported strong Q2 results and raised its full-year guidance, but the orforglipron news overshadowed the earnings beat.

data.title: Kontoor Brands beats Q2 expectations, lifts FY25 guidance despite tariff headwinds
data.description:
Kontoor Brands (NYSE:KTB) opened with a 12% gain on Thursday as the popularity of the company’s Wrangler jeans and Helly Hansen merchandise resulted in better-than-expected second quarter  results and improved outlook for FY25.

“Our strong second quarter results were driven by better-than-expected organic revenue growth, gross margin expansion, operating efficiency and cash generation, as well as a stronger-than-expected contribution from Helly Hansen,” said Scott Baxter, CEO of Kontoor Brands. 

Kontoor Brands (NYSE:KTB) completed its acquisition of outdoor clothing and sports equipment manufacturer Helly Hansen in June, with an expectation that the Norwegian brand would add $425M to consolidated FY25 revenue and $0.20 to EPS.

The parent company of Wrangler and Lee jeans earned an adjusted profit of $1.21 per share, up 23% year-over-year and $0.38 better than expected. Excluding the Helly Hansen acquisition, earnings increased 36% to $1.33. Gross margin expanded 160 basis points to 46.3%.

Sales improved 8.4% to $658M, $24.3M better than anticipated as increased sales for Wrangler and the inclusion of $29M from Helly Hansen offset a 6% drop in Lee sales.

For FY25, Kontoor Brands (NYSE:KTB) raised its outlook which includes the impact from increased investments and the absorption of higher tariffs, specifically 30% reciprocal tariff on China and 20% on other countries from which Kontoor sources merchandise.

“While we will continue to manage the business prudently in light of the environment, the third quarter is off to an encouraging start, and we enter the second half of the year from a position of strength,” Baxter added.

Revenue for FY25 is now expected to be in the range of $3.09B to $3.12B, representing growth of 19% to 20%, versus prior outlook of 17% to 19% growth to $3.06B to $3.09B. This compares to the consensus estimate of $3.05B.

Adjusted gross margin is expected to increase 100 basis points to 46.1%, while adjusted earnings are now expected to be approximately $5.45 per share, versus a range of $5.40 to $5.50 per share, previously, and above the estimated $5.24.

For the current quarter, Kontoor Brands (KTB) expects revenue to increase 8% year-over-year to $855M and earnings to decrease 2 cents from last year to $1.35 per share. This compares to estimates of $862.5M and $1.62, respectively.

data.title: Becton Dickinson raises EPS guidance amid strategic separation and innovation launches
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Becton, Dickinson and Company (BDX) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO Thomas E. Polen reported sequential improvement in growth, stating "We accelerated commercial initiatives and increased our organic growth trajectory through market headwinds. We continued our strong track record of executing BD Excellence to drive gross margin upside." He highlighted the announcement of a definitive agreement to separate the Biosciences and Diagnostics business through a transaction with Waters, expected to unlock value for shareholders. Polen noted, "Revenue grew 8.5% to $5.5 billion or 3% organic. New BD organic growth was 4%." He further added, "Q3 adjusted gross margin of 54.8%, up 50 basis points year-over-year. Adjusted operating margin of 25.8%, up 60 basis points year-over-year and supported delivering $3.68 in adjusted diluted EPS, each exceeded our expectations."
  • Polen emphasized strategic initiatives, mentioning, "We announced that we entered into an agreement to combine our Biosciences and Diagnostic Solutions business with Waters through a tax-efficient Reverse Morris Trust." He also introduced Brooke Story as head of integration and separation management, citing her experience as President of the Diagnostics business.
  • CFO Christopher J. DelOrefice stated, "Revenues of $5.5 billion grew 8.5% or 3% organic. New BD organic growth was 4%. Regionally, total company organic growth was led by performance in the U.S. and Greater Asia outside of China, partially offset by China." He added, "We delivered strong adjusted gross margin of 54.8% and adjusted operating margin of 25.8%, which increased by 50 and 60 basis points year-over-year, respectively. Gross margin expansion continued to be primarily fueled by momentum in BD Excellence, driven by manufacturing productivity, waste improvement and network optimization."

Outlook

  • The company reaffirmed its organic revenue guidance range for the year and raised its earnings guidance by $0.18 at the midpoint. Polen said, "We reaffirmed our organic revenue guide range for the year and raised our earnings guidance $0.18 at the midpoint."
  • DelOrefice stated, "We raised our adjusted EPS guidance by $0.18 at the midpoint to a range of $14.30 to $14.45. This reflects growth of about 9.4% at the midpoint, which is an increase of approximately 1.4% compared to our prior guidance." He added, "We remain on track to deliver our goal of 25% operating margin by 2025."
  • The company expects a full year 2026 tariff impact of around $275 million, an improvement compared to initial expectations.

Financial Results

  • Revenue for Q3 was $5.5 billion, with organic growth at 3% and new BD organic growth at 4%.
  • Adjusted gross margin was 54.8%, and adjusted operating margin was 25.8%.
  • Adjusted diluted EPS was $3.68, growing 5.1% year-over-year.
  • Year-to-date free cash flows were approximately $1.7 billion, increasing sequentially by $1 billion.
  • Net leverage ended the quarter at 2.8x, with progress towards a 2.5x long-term target.
  • The company expects to complete its $1 billion share buyback by the end of September.

Q&A

  • Patrick Wood, Morgan Stanley, asked about RemainCo's growth framework and mid-single-digit plus growth potential. Polen responded, "There's nothing different that we would expect fundamentally from those. I think as we -- as you also mentioned, we saw very strong growth in APM ahead of our deal model, 13% growth for the quarter."
  • Frederick Allen Wise, Stifel, questioned Q4 margin dynamics and headwinds. DelOrefice replied, "There'll be a slight sequential step down quarter-to-quarter, and it's largely driven by just the timing of our investments... we're actually fueling the business with more investment to continue to fuel those areas of momentum."
  • Lawrence H. Biegelsen, Wells Fargo, inquired about margin outlook post-separation and fiscal 2026 impacts from tariffs. DelOrefice stated, "There will actually be very difference in terms of operating margin pre-separation and post separation. So that's within a reasonable range, plus or minus. So you should see a healthy margin come out, plus you're going to continue to get the benefit of BD Excellence carrying through."
  • Travis Lee Steed, Bank of America, asked about growth outlook and capital deployment post-separation. Polen explained, "We're obviously very focused on executing in '25 and chopping wood through the balance of the year, but I could give a little bit of -- and it's too early to talk details about '26, but maybe just give a little bit of thoughts to share related to that."
  • Robert Justin Marcus, JPMorgan, asked about urology growth and SG&A investments. Polen confirmed, "Now urology is continuing. There's nothing onetime there at all. It's a continuation of the trends...really driven by innovation on the UCC side." DelOrefice added, "We want to keep investing in both innovation and commercial execution."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was inquisitive and focused on clarification around margin impacts, future growth, and separation details, with a neutral to slightly positive sentiment as they recognized strong execution but pressed for clarity on future risks.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone in prepared remarks, using phrases such as "We are pleased with the performance" and "We feel really good about that," but showed some caution and prudence when discussing potential macro headwinds and tariff impacts.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst skepticism about macro challenges and mitigation strategies was less pronounced, and management's confidence appeared strengthened by improved sequential performance and raised guidance.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from a defensive stance in Q2 (addressing missed topline expectations and macro challenges) to an assertive tone in Q3, with raised EPS guidance and reaffirmed revenue targets.
  • Strategic focus advanced from mitigation and stabilization (tariffs, supply chain, research funding) to active separation of the Biosciences and Diagnostics business and new product launches.
  • Analysts in Q3 shifted from probing missed targets and mitigation to exploring growth sustainability and the impact of the separation.
  • Key metric changes included improved revenue growth, margin expansion, and a higher EPS outlook. Management expressed increased confidence in operational execution and innovation-driven growth.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted persistent market headwinds in China, volatility in subsegments such as vaccines, and ongoing tariff impacts as ongoing risks.
  • The company continues to mitigate tariffs through supply chain optimization and sourcing changes, with teams deployed to "keep whittling that down."
  • Analysts raised concerns about the sustainability of margin expansion, the permanence of new investments in SG&A, and the impact of tariffs on future financials.

Final Takeaway

Becton Dickinson delivered sequential improvements in growth, expanded margins, and exceeded earnings expectations in the third quarter, driven by successful commercial initiatives and strategic investments. The company reaffirmed its revenue guidance and raised its EPS outlook, underscoring confidence in operational momentum and innovation launches. The pending separation of the Biosciences and Diagnostics business and continued focus on margin expansion through BD Excellence set the stage for BD’s next phase as a pure-play medtech leader, while proactive tariff mitigation and targeted investments support a robust foundation for long-term value creation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
